title: Architecture analysis: package detection and coupling metrics (Ca/Ce/instability)
description:
Detect high-level project structure (packages and architectural layers) and compute coupling metrics between them. Useful for refactor planning, codebase health monitoring, and PR review.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's architecture/coupling/package commands. The metrics (afferent coupling, efferent coupling, instability) are Robert C. Martin classics — well-established and immediately legible to seasoned engineers.

**What we already have:**
- Cargo.toml discovery via cargo::discover_crates → CrateInfo with name + path.
- file_deps table with from_file_id → to_file_id edges.
- Both feed naturally into per-crate rollups; no new parsing required.

**What this adds:**
- arch_packages table: id, name, path, source ('manifest'|'directory'), language, version, manifest_path.
- arch_file_packages: file_id → package_id mapping.
- arch_package_deps: package_id → package_id with import_count, rolled up from file_deps.
- arch_coupling: per-package Ca, Ce, instability = Ce / (Ca + Ce).

Definitions:
- Ca (afferent coupling): how many other packages depend on this one. Higher = more stable / load-bearing.
- Ce (efferent coupling): how many packages this one depends on. Higher = more unstable.
- Instability: 0 = maximally stable (everyone depends on it, it depends on nothing); 1 = maximally unstable.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::get_packages() -> Vec<Package>
- Tethys::get_coupling_metrics(package_name: Option<&str>) -> Vec<CouplingEntry>
- Tethys::get_package_dependencies(package_id) -> Vec<PackageDep>

**CLI:**
- tethys architecture [--format table|json]
- tethys coupling [--package NAME] [--sort instability|ca|ce|name]
- tethys package <NAME> [--no-files]

**Layer detection (lower priority, follow-up):**
KiroGraph also classifies files into layers (api/service/data/ui/shared) by path patterns. Could be a v2; the package-level metrics are the high-value primary feature.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Schema migration adds arch_packages, arch_file_packages, arch_package_deps, arch_coupling
- [ ] Architecture analysis runs as a phase during indexing (only when enabled — opt-in flag)
- [ ] CLI: tethys architecture, tethys coupling, tethys package
- [ ] Coupling metrics correctly compute Ca, Ce, instability for the rivets workspace itself
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_coupling and tethys_architecture (sibling rivets-o4re)
- [ ] Documented in tethys README
- [ ] Tests verify metrics on a multi-crate fixture workspace

title: refs table omits free-function identifiers used as values and macro-token-tree identifiers
description:
The Rust reference extractor produces refs ONLY for call_expression, struct_expression, and type_identifier nodes (src/languages/rust.rs:165-250). Two real categories of free-function/identifier references are therefore never recorded:

1. Function-as-value: a function referenced WITHOUT calling it — passed as a callback or stored (iter.map(node_text), let f = validate;). This is a plain identifier in argument/value position (not a call_expression, not a type_identifier), so no ref is emitted.
2. Macro token-tree identifiers: identifiers (including calls) that appear only inside a macro invocation's token tree are not parsed into call_expression nodes, so references that exist only at a macro call site are missed.

Impact: a function used ONLY as a value (never called via call_expression) has zero inbound call-refs/call_edges, so it is a FALSE POSITIVE for the dead-code finder (tethys-dvsw) and undercounted by hotspots (tethys-7p54) — which is why those depend on this issue.

Scope note: this is NOT the same as bare CALL foo(), which IS captured correctly (see closed tethys-zp2j). And it is distinct from the reference_name-nulling queryability bug (tethys-6rlu). Category 1 (fn-as-value) is the higher-value half; category 2 (macro tokens) is lower-value and higher false-positive — split if needed.

Provenance: 'Missing refs: fn-as-value and macro token-tree identifiers invisible' from the June 2026 indexing-strategy review; never previously filed (an earlier note cited a phantom ID tethys-cfme).
design: tree-sitter-rust: (1) fn-as-value is an identifier node in expression/argument position whose parent is NOT a call_expression callee and NOT a scoped_identifier — needs a textual/symbol-table guard to avoid recording every identifier as a ref. (2) macro identifiers live under token_tree inside macro_invocation; the extractor currently does not descend into token_tree. Both are higher-false-positive than zp2j's bare-call case, so apply conservative guards (suppressions, not accusations) per the project's error posture.
acceptance_criteria:
* fn-as-value: indexing a workspace where an in-crate free function foo is passed as a value (e.g. iter.map(foo) or let g = foo;) yields >= 1 refs row with reference_name='foo' (kind distinguishing value-use from call is acceptable).
* macro-token identifier: an explicit decision is recorded — either a refs row is produced for an in-crate symbol referenced only inside a macro token tree, or it is documented as out-of-scope with rationale.
* Existing bare-call (tethys-zp2j), method-call, and qualified-call extraction is unchanged (regression test).
* Conservative: no spurious refs for shadowed locals / non-symbol identifiers.
notes: Filed 2026-06-30 during the zp2j dedup review. Originally framed as a zp2j sibling; reframed to stand alone after zp2j was verified invalid and closed. fn-as-value gap confirmed by reading the extractor dispatch (no arm records a standalone identifier used as a value).

title: tethys: resolve_super_path uses filesystem-walk semantics, diverges from Rust super:: scoping
design:
Two candidate fixes:

(a) Build a module-tree map at index time: for each `<basename>.rs` adjacent to a `<basename>/` directory, record that the .rs file is the parent-module file of the subdirectory's contents. Then `resolve_super_path` walks the module tree instead of the filesystem.

(b) Special-case the parent-module pattern in resolve_super_path: when `current_file.parent()` contains a sibling `<dir_basename>.rs` (e.g. for `src/parent/child.rs`, check whether `src/parent.rs` exists), treat that .rs file as the parent module and resolve `super::X` relative to *its* directory (which is `src/parent/`, not `src/`).

Option (b) is narrower and matches the common-case Rust idiom (parent.rs + parent/ pattern). Option (a) handles edge cases like `parent/mod.rs` + sibling files. Either way, `resolve_self_path` may need parallel review since it's the inverse semantics question.

Risk: any test or workspace currently relying on the filesystem-walk semantics breaks. The rivets workspace itself doesn't seem to use this shape (otherwise PR #74's rivets-workspace re-index would have surfaced differently).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] resolve_super_path returns `src/parent/X.rs` for ref `super::X` from `src/parent/child.rs` when `src/parent.rs` exists
- [ ] Test in pass2_qualified_paths.rs (or new test file) pins the Rust-spec semantics
- [ ] Existing rivets workspace re-index post-fix: no phantom-rate regression (claim 7 of rivets-3d0s fence holds)
- [ ] Update self_and_super_paths_resolve_via_as_written's docstring to remove the divergence-documentation language (or replace with a comment that says "this is what Rust does, this is what we do, and they now agree")
- [ ] resolve_self_path reviewed for parallel issues; either confirmed correct or follow-up issue filed

title: Pass 2: self-crate qualified call crate::X() does not resolve to in-crate X
description:
A qualified call through the crate root, crate::helper(), to an in-crate free function helper (defined in the crate root lib.rs) is NOT resolved in Pass 2: the ref stays unresolved (symbol_id NULL) with reference_name='crate::helper'.

Contrast (both work): a BARE helper() via 'use crate::helper;' resolves; and a submodule-qualified helper::do_thing_q() resolves (see tests/pass2_qualified_paths.rs::submodule_qualified_call_resolves). So the gap is specifically the leading 'crate' segment / crate-root absolute path — resolve.rs::qualified_module_fallback + resolver::resolve_module_path do not map 'crate' to the crate root.

Repro:
  src/lib.rs:  pub mod b; pub fn helper() {}
  src/b.rs:    use crate::helper; pub fn use_it() { helper(); crate::helper(); }
  After index:
    SELECT symbol_id, reference_name FROM refs r JOIN files f ON r.file_id=f.id
      WHERE f.path LIKE '%b.rs';
    -> bare helper() : symbol_id set (resolved)
    -> crate::helper(): symbol_id NULL, reference_name='crate::helper' (UNRESOLVED)

Impact: callers/impact/reachability under-count symbols invoked via crate:: paths. Also surfaced in tethys-6rlu: the refs_named view keys this unresolved ref by 'crate::helper', so a bare-name query (WHERE name='helper') misses it. tethys-6rlu documents that as a limitation; resolving crate:: here would remove it (the view then keys the ref by symbols.name='helper', no view change needed).
acceptance_criteria:
* After indexing the repro, the crate::helper() ref resolves: symbol_id points to the in-crate helper symbol and reference_name is NULL.
* tethys callers helper lists the crate::helper() call site.
* Existing submodule-qualified and bare-call resolution unchanged (regression).
* A fixture pins crate::X() (X in crate root) resolving.

title: tethys: streaming compute_all_dependencies re-inserts file_deps from orphan files (deleted-from-disk but still in DB)
description:
Streaming-mode indexing (IndexOptions::with_streaming()) calls compute_all_dependencies (crates/tethys/src/indexing.rs:943) which iterates self.db.list_all_files() — the FULL set of files in the DB, including files that have been deleted from disk since their last index. For each such orphan, it loads the stale stored imports + refs and calls compute_dependencies_from_stored, which re-inserts file_deps rows with the orphan''s file_id as from_file_id.

Downstream queries (coupling Ce/Ca, callers, cycles, impact analysis) then see the orphan as a real source of cross-file edges, producing phantom contributions.

**Asymmetries — which paths are affected:**

| Path | Status |
|---|---|
| tethys index --rebuild (any mode) | Not affected — db.reset() wipes DB; orphans gone |
| tethys index non-streaming (default) | Not affected — compute_dependencies runs per disk-file in the parse loop; orphans never seen |
| tethys index streaming | **Affected** |

**Root cause:** index_with_options has no orphan-cleanup pass. db/files.rs:145-146 only DELETEs symbols/imports when an EXISTING file is RE-indexed (still on disk). Files deleted from disk are never processed, so no DELETE fires for them. FileChange::Deleted is detected in reindex.rs:122 but only by get_stale_files() (observation API, not called from indexing).

**How discovered:** Gemini code review on PR #65 flagged "compute_all_dependencies may re-calculate dependencies for stale entries (files in DB but deleted from disk)." Initial round-1 verdict was "reject (not a real bug)" on cascade-cleanup grounds; pressure-test revealed the cascade only applies to re-indexed files, not orphans. See .rivets-lcb6/review-decisions-round-1.md for the corrected verdict.

**Not caused by rivets-lcb6.** Pre-existing in streaming mode. rivets-lcb6''s clear_all_file_deps even partially mitigates by wiping file_deps each run, but compute_all_dependencies immediately re-inserts from orphans.

**Likely fix shape:** before compute_all_dependencies runs, delete file rows whose disk path doesn''t resolve (and let FK cascades clean up dependents). Either:
1. New cleanup_orphan_files that classifies via the same staleness check as reindex.rs::classify_indexed_file and DELETEs orphan rows, called from index_with_options before resolver/dep passes.
2. Filter compute_all_dependencies to skip files whose path doesn''t exist on disk (lighter touch, leaves orphan rows in files table).

Option 1 is cleaner — once orphan files are gone from the DB, all downstream queries are correct without per-callsite filtering.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Regression test: index a 2-file workspace in streaming mode, delete one file from disk, re-index (no --rebuild). Verify (a) the orphan no longer appears in list_all_files, OR (b) file_deps contains no rows with from_file_id == orphan's id.
- [ ] Equivalent test for non-streaming mode confirms the bug doesn't manifest there (regression fence).
- [ ] No perf regression: indexing a workspace with N files and 0 orphans takes the same time before and after.

title: tethys: non-transactional clear in index_with_options leaves DB degraded on mid-pipeline panic
description:
Both clear_all_call_edges (indexing.rs:424) and clear_all_file_deps (indexing.rs:138, added in rivets-lcb6) run outside any explicit transaction. If the indexing pipeline panics or is interrupted between the clear and the subsequent re-population:

- file_deps is empty (or partially populated)
- call_edges is empty (if the panic happens after line 424)

Until the next complete index run, downstream queries (coupling, callers, cycles, impact analysis) operate on degraded data.

This is pre-existing for call_edges; rivets-lcb6 makes file_deps adopt the same pattern (the alternative — leaving file_deps stale — has worse properties). So this issue documents a known risk shared by both, not a regression introduced by lcb6.

Surfaced by claude-review on PR #65.

Likely fix shape: wrap the clear + populate work in a transaction so partial failure rolls back. Connection-level: BEGIN / COMMIT around the relevant indexing.rs section. SQLite WAL mode supports this; the question is what 'partial failure' means in the current architecture (batch_writer commits in chunks, so wrapping the whole pipeline in one transaction may not be feasible without changes there).

Out of scope here; file before fix work begins so the design considers it.

Reference acceptance criteria for the eventual fix:
- Test: index a workspace, abort mid-pipeline (panic injection), assert file_deps + call_edges are EITHER both populated from prior state OR both empty — no half-populated states.
- Test: under normal operation, no perf regression compared to current non-transactional pattern.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] file_deps and call_edges remain consistent (both old-state or both new-state) across pipeline abort
- [ ] No significant indexing perf regression
- [ ] Both clear_all_file_deps and clear_all_call_edges share the same transactional discipline

title: PR #60 polish backlog: cleanup items deferred from review rounds
description:
Collection of low-priority cleanup items surfaced during PR #60's third review round. Each is real but represents diminishing returns relative to the work already done; bundle them for a future hygiene pass.

**Items:**

1. Remove the now-redundant ''suggestions_capped_at_five'' test in cli/coupling.rs — the new ''suggestions_capped_at_five_returns_first_five_by_input_order'' is a strict superset. Both currently exist and assert the same length on identical inputs.

2. Add an ''add_file'' helper to ''package_coupling_tests'' (it already exists in ''repopulate_architecture_tests''). The new sort-order test in particular repeats the inline ''upsert_file'' pattern 7 times.

3. Strengthen ''get_package_coupling_returns_err_for_corrupt_target_source'': currently asserts only ''is_err()''. Add assertions that the error is the expected variant (''Error::Internal'') and that the message contains the corrupt source value (''totally-bogus'') so a future change that drops diagnostic context would fail the test.

4. Corrupt-source tests use hardcoded package ''id=1,2,3'' and rely on ''PRAGMA ignore_check_constraints'' silently succeeding. Add a verification ''SELECT'' after the corrupt INSERT to confirm the bad row landed, so a silent PRAGMA failure produces a clear diagnostic at the injection point instead of a confusing downstream assertion mismatch. Optionally switch the inserts to use ''INSERT ... RETURNING id'' or query back ids rather than hardcoding.

5. The ''completed_path_writes_nothing_to_stdout'' assertion message embeds the ''rivets-tuph'' ticket reference. Move it to a ''// TODO(rivets-tuph)'' comment above the assertion so the failure message stays clean and the editorial intent stays out of test output.

6. Tighten the ''#[expect(clippy::cast_precision_loss, reason = ...)]'' annotation on ''CouplingMetrics::instability'' to explicitly say ''the annotated cast is ; the numerator uses  which is infallible widening, exempt from the lint.''

7. Add a reciprocal cross-reference: ''Package.name'' field doc should point at ''PackageId'' (''recommended stable identity; see [] for why the numeric id is unsuitable for that purpose''). Currently the ''PackageId → name'' direction is documented but not the reverse.

8. The ''none_writes_nothing'' assertion is bare ''assert!(buf.is_empty())'' with no contract message. Add an explanation so a failure tells a future maintainer what the ''None'' arm represents.

Discovered during third-round PR #60 review (the 6 specialist reviewers ran on commits a4949bb..HEAD).

title: tethys: rename format_uri to absolute_path_to_uri
description:
format_uri's rustdoc says 'caller must pass an absolute, canonicalized path' — that precondition lives only in prose. A CanonicalPath newtype would be overkill for two private callsites, but the rename converts a hidden precondition into a name a reviewer can't miss.

Trivial: rename the fn, update the one caller in path_to_uri, update the five format_uri_* test names if desired (or leave them as the shorter form).

If the function ever goes pub(crate), revisit with CanonicalPath. Defer until that day.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (type-design-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:

- [ ] Update caller in path_to_uri
- [ ] Update doc comments referencing the old name

title: Test topology mapping
notes: Most primitives exist. Main work is extracting #[test] attributes during parsing and adding a simple query.

title: Resolution NULLs refs.reference_name — name-based queries over refs are silently empty for resolved symbols
description:
Pass-2 resolution sets reference_name=NULL once a ref resolves to an in-crate symbol (src/db/references.rs:157: UPDATE refs SET symbol_id=?2, reference_name=NULL WHERE id=?1). Initial storage also only populates reference_name when symbol_id IS NULL (src/indexing.rs:779, src/batch_writer.rs:329).

Net effect: refs.reference_name is populated ONLY for UNRESOLVED references. Every reference that resolves to an in-crate symbol keeps just a numeric symbol_id and has reference_name=NULL.

Consequence (the real bug): name-based SQL over the refs table is silently incomplete. SELECT ... FROM refs WHERE reference_name='foo' returns 0 for any in-crate symbol foo that resolved — even though the ref rows exist. The only correct name query is a join: JOIN symbols s ON r.symbol_id=s.id WHERE s.name='foo'. External/ad-hoc SQL consumers have no way to know this and get vacuous results that look like 'no references'.

This is the actual root cause behind the now-closed tethys-zp2j: a kiro-control-center plan-lint gate filtering refs.reference_name='make_service' found the refs 'vacuous' and fell back to the imports table. The refs were present (resolved, reference_name NULL); the gate measured the wrong column.

Verified (self-index): bare free fn node_text => 0 rows WHERE reference_name='node_text', but 60 refs + 37 call_edges via JOIN symbols on symbol_id (kind='call'). Globally: 1884 resolved call-refs carry reference_name=NULL; 7673 unresolved carry a name.
design:
DECISION (falsifiable-design 2026-06-30): add an additive SQL view 'refs_named' (LEFT JOIN refs->symbols, COALESCE(reference_name, symbols.name) AS name). NOT option 1 (overload reference_name).

Rationale (subtractive sweep): overloading reference_name to always carry the name breaks two consumers that assume 'reference_name populated => unresolved': panic_points.rs:51,103 (WHERE reference_name IN ('unwrap','expect') with no symbol_id guard -> in-crate unwrap/expect gain false panic sites) and indexing.rs:1003 compute_dependencies_from_stored (resolved same-file names leak into refs_set -> phantom file_deps). The view leaves reference_name semantics untouched (no consumer changes) and computes name live (no rename staleness).

Probe+oracle agreed (5 fns, counts 1-59). Cheapest falsifier passed against the real view on a fresh index. Full design + 7-claim falsification table: .tethys-6rlu/design.md. Implementation: add CREATE VIEW IF NOT EXISTS refs_named to src/db/schema.rs; document refs_named as the name-query surface.

NOTE: on-disk index was stale during probing -> every regression fence must build its OWN index from a fixture, never query an ambient DB.
acceptance_criteria:
* After indexing + Pass-2 resolution, a ref to an in-crate symbol foo is retrievable by name from refs WITHOUT manually joining symbols (reference_name stays populated, or a documented view/column provides it).
* A regression fixture pins the node_text-style case: name-query count over refs == symbol_id-join count for a known in-crate free function.
* Storage decision (denormalize vs view) recorded in the issue.
* Existing resolved-ref consumers (get_callers, call_edges, dead-code) unaffected; no double-counting.
notes: Closed: Implemented on feat/tethys-6rlu-refs-named: additive refs_named SQL view (COALESCE(reference_name, symbols.name) over LEFT JOIN), auto-applied on every Index::open. 7 regression fences (C1-C7); full suite green, clippy clean, prove-it oracle 5/5. C1 narrowed to resolved+bare-unresolved (unresolved qualified refs keyed by qualified path — tethys-3i35); C5 folded into the root invariant. reference_name semantics untouched -> no consumer change.

title: Discriminated NotFound variants in tethys::Error (e.g., PackageNotFound)
description:
Currently tethys::Error::NotFound(String) carries an ad-hoc payload format like 'package: foo', 'file: bar', 'symbol: baz'. The string content is a de facto protocol for distinguishing what was not found, but nothing in the type enforces or documents it.

This becomes a problem when rivets-mcp adds a tethys_coupling tool. If MCP needs to react differently to 'package not found' vs 'file not found' (e.g., suggest re-indexing for the former, suggest checking the path for the latter), it has to parse the string payload — a brittle implicit contract.

**Fix:** Introduce discriminated variants on tethys::Error:
- PackageNotFound(String)
- (existing NotFound(String) stays as catch-all for legacy callers)

Update get_package_coupling and cli/coupling.rs::run_detail_to to use PackageNotFound.

**Why deferred:** Low urgency today because (a) no MCP consumer of get_package_coupling exists yet, (b) the string format isn't being parsed anywhere, (c) main.rs renders both variants the same way. The right time to fix is just before rivets-o4re wires up tethys_coupling.

Discovered during the durability review pass on PR #60.

title: Pre-computed call graph edges table
description:
Store explicit caller→callee edges in a dedicated table for faster graph queries.

**Current approach:**
- `get_callers` joins references → symbols → files
- Complex query with multiple joins
- Re-computed on every query

**Drift's approach:**
- Dedicated `call_edges` table: `(caller_id, callee_id, call_count)`
- Pre-computed during indexing
- Simple lookups for callers/callees

**Proposed schema:**
```sql
CREATE TABLE call_edges (
    caller_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    callee_symbol_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    call_count INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
    PRIMARY KEY (caller_symbol_id, callee_symbol_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (caller_symbol_id) REFERENCES symbols(id),
    FOREIGN KEY (callee_symbol_id) REFERENCES symbols(id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_call_edges_callee ON call_edges(callee_symbol_id);
```

**Benefits:**
- O(1) lookup for "who calls X?" instead of join-heavy query
- Enables efficient reachability analysis
- Pre-computed call counts for hotspot detection

**Trade-off:**
- Slightly larger database
- Need to update edges during incremental re-index

title: Consolidate three copies of workspace_with_files test helper
description: Three independent copies of the workspace_with_files test fixture helper exist in the tethys integration test suite. PR #63 (rivets-6aoc) had to update all three with the same Cargo.toml auto-write logic; future test-infrastructure changes will keep paying this triplicate cost.
design:
## Current state

Three near-identical `workspace_with_files` functions:

1. `crates/tethys/tests/common/mod.rs` (shared, intended canonical location)
2. `crates/tethys/tests/indexing.rs` (local copy, 77 callers in this one file)
3. `crates/tethys/tests/test_topology.rs` (local copy)

All three now (post-PR #63) have similar auto-write-default-Cargo.toml logic with slight string differences:
- common writes "test_workspace"
- indexing writes "test_workspace"
- test_topology writes "test_topology"

## Folds in S2 from PR #63 review

The Cargo.toml exact-string match in the helpers (`files.iter().any(|(p, _)| *p == "Cargo.toml")`) is fragile: a caller passing "./Cargo.toml" would trigger a silent double-write. Normalize via `Path::file_name()` once during consolidation.

## Proposed work

1. Make `tests/common/mod.rs::workspace_with_files` the canonical helper.
2. Delete the local copies in `tests/indexing.rs` and `tests/test_topology.rs`.
3. Update all callers in those files to `use common::workspace_with_files;` (add `mod common;`).
4. Normalize Cargo.toml detection in the canonical helper via `Path::file_name()` rather than exact-string comparison.
5. Verify all integration tests still pass.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Single workspace_with_files in tests/common/mod.rs
- [ ] indexing.rs and test_topology.rs use the common helper via mod common
- [ ] Cargo.toml detection uses Path::file_name(), not exact-string match
- [ ] cargo nextest run -p tethys passes
- [ ] cargo clippy + cargo fmt clean

title: Add rayon for parallel file parsing
description:
Use rayon to parallelize file parsing during indexing. Currently tethys processes files sequentially, using only one CPU core.

**Drift's pattern:**
```rust
// Parallel parsing with rayon
let results: Vec<_> = files
    .par_iter()
    .filter_map(|file| process_file(file).ok())
    .collect();
```

**Key considerations:**
- Parse in parallel, write to SQLite sequentially (SQLite doesn't like concurrent writes)
- Use MPSC channel to send parsed data to a background writer thread
- Batch SQLite writes (100 files per transaction) to amortize overhead
- Use atomic counters for thread-safe progress tracking

**Expected improvement:**
- 1,000 files: ~2-3s → ~0.5-1s
- 10,000 files: ~20-30s → ~3-5s
- Full CPU utilization instead of single core

**Implementation steps:**
1. Add `rayon = "1.10"` to Cargo.toml
2. Create `BatchWriter` struct with MPSC channel
3. Refactor `index_workspace` to use `par_iter()` for Pass 1
4. Keep Pass 2 (import resolution) and Pass 3 (LSP) sequential

title: tethys LSP: verify or extend path_to_uri handling for UNC paths
description: rivets-714v fixes path_to_uri's \?\ extended-length prefix bug for drive-letter paths (C:\...). UNC paths (\\server\share\...) follow a different shape and may need explicit handling: canonicalize() returns them as \\?\UNC\server\share\... on Windows, and the RFC 8089 file URI form for UNC is ambiguous between authority-style (file://server/share/...) and path-style (file:////server/share/...).
design:
## Why deferred from rivets-714v

The rivets-714v fix is bounded to drive-letter paths (the reported and reproduced bug case). UNC paths:

1. Are rare in production for the rivets workspace and typical Cargo users
2. Have ambiguous RFC 8089 mapping (authority vs path-style)
3. Would require a separate decision about which form rust-analyzer accepts
4. Need their own probe + oracle to verify behavior

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a probe that constructs a fixture on a UNC mount (or simulates one via Path manipulation)
- [ ] Verify what URI shape rust-analyzer accepts for UNC paths
- [ ] Extend path_to_uri to produce that shape correctly
- [ ] Add a regression test covering the UNC case

## Trigger condition for promoting priority

If a user reports tethys --lsp failing on a UNC-mounted workspace OR if the test_topology suite ever exercises a UNC fixture and surfaces the gap. Otherwise P4 is appropriate.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] UNC path probe + oracle added (mirrors .rivets-714v/probe.py shape)
- [ ] path_to_uri's behavior on UNC paths verified against rust-analyzer's expectations
- [ ] Regression test for UNC shape added
- [ ] Documented in path_to_uri's rustdoc what URI form is produced for UNC inputs

title: tethys: implement depth-limited transitive impact analysis
description: The --depth flag in impact analysis is accepted but not implemented. Currently, transitive analysis always explores the full dependency graph. Implement depth limiting to control how many levels of indirection to follow.

title: Complete C# unsafe modifier and generic parameter extraction
description: Two TODO sites in languages/csharp.rs: line 1036 (detect unsafe modifier) and line 1038 (extract type parameters/generics). Currently hardcoded to false/None.

title: Store unresolved references with symbol_id = NULL
description:
Currently we skip references we can't resolve. Instead, store them with `symbol_id = NULL` and enough context to resolve later.

This requires allowing nullable symbol_id in the refs table and updating `store_references()` to handle unresolved refs.

title: Implement content hashing for file change detection
description: The content_hash field exists in the schema and types but is always passed as None. Implement actual content hashing to enable content-based change detection alongside mtime. This would allow skipping re-indexing when mtime changes but content has not. Three TODO sites: lib.rs (2) and batch_writer.rs (1).
notes: Closed: Duplicate of rivets-3l14 (older, describes the same content_hash TODOs in lib.rs and batch_writer.rs).

title: Surprising connections: cross-file edges scored by file-path distance
description:
Find non-obvious cross-file connections — direct edges between symbols in structurally distant files. High-score pairs indicate unexpected coupling worth investigating.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_surprising tool. The clever insight: rank cross-file edges by path_distance(source_file, target_file) * edge_kind_weight to surface coupling that a human reviewer would never spot in casual inspection.

**Scoring:**
- Path distance: number of directory hops between source and target file paths.
- Edge kind weights (suggested initial values): calls=1.0, references=0.8, type_of=0.7, instantiates=0.7, imports=0.3 (low — imports are expected).
- Score = path_distance * weight; return top-N unique source-target pairs.

**Why this depends on rivets-puyl:**
The call_edges table currently has no kind column. Surprising-connection scoring needs to weight different edge kinds differently, so we need rivets-puyl (Add reference kind tracking to call_edges table) to land first. Without it, we can only score on path distance, which is much weaker signal.

**Implementation:**
- Public API: Tethys::find_surprising_connections(limit) -> Vec<SurprisingEntry { source, target, edge_kind, score }>
- CLI: tethys surprising [--limit N] [--json]
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] find_surprising_connections() API method
- [ ] CLI subcommand with --limit and --json
- [ ] Path-distance helper: count distinct directory components between two relative paths
- [ ] Edge-kind weights configurable or at least documented as constants
- [ ] Filters out same-file edges
- [ ] Unit tests on a multi-directory fixture workspace

title: Track failed files in WriteStats for debugging
description:
Currently `WriteStats` only tracks successful writes. When files fail to write, that information is logged but lost from the final result.

**Current struct:**
```rust
pub struct WriteStats {
    pub files_written: usize,
    pub symbols_written: usize,
    pub references_written: usize,
    pub batches_committed: usize,
}
```

**Suggested addition:**
```rust
pub struct WriteStats {
    // ... existing fields ...
    /// Files that failed to write with their error messages
    pub failed_files: Vec<(PathBuf, String)>,
}
```

**Benefits:**
- Users can see which files need attention without grepping logs
- Enables retry logic for failed files
- Makes partial success explicit rather than hidden

**Trade-off:** Increases memory usage if many files fail (unlikely in practice).

title: Tethys: Codebase Intelligence Engine
description:
Improvements inspired by analyzing the Drift codebase. Drift is a mature codebase intelligence platform that handles 10K+ file codebases efficiently.

Key patterns from Drift:
- Rayon for parallel file parsing
- Streaming SQLite writes via MPSC channel
- Pre-computed call graph edges
- MCP server integration for AI agents
- Reachability analysis for security auditing
- Test topology for CI integration

These improvements would make tethys production-ready for large codebases.

title: Method calls resolve by name only: x.unwrap() on external types misattributed to a same-named in-crate method
description:
Method-call resolution matches on the method NAME without receiver-type information, so a call like x.unwrap() where x is an external/std type (e.g. Option) resolves to an in-crate method of the same name when one exists (e.g. an inherent Thing::unwrap).

Effects:
1. Fabricated call edge: an unrelated caller appears to call the in-crate Thing::unwrap (over-attribution in callers/impact/reachability).
2. Hidden external call: the genuine external .unwrap() gets a symbol_id and its reference_name is nulled, so panic-points (WHERE reference_name IN ('unwrap','expect')) reports 0 — a false negative.

Repro:
  src/lib.rs:
    pub struct Thing; impl Thing { pub fn unwrap(&self) {} }
    pub fn use_external() { let x: Option<i32> = Some(1); x.unwrap(); }
    pub fn use_internal()  { let t = Thing; t.unwrap(); }
  After index: BOTH unwrap call refs have the SAME symbol_id (the in-crate Thing::unwrap); Panic Points Analysis

title: tethys: track parent_symbol_id for nested symbols
description: When indexing symbols, parent_symbol_id is always set to None. Implement proper parent symbol tracking so nested symbols (e.g., methods inside structs/impls) have their parent relationship recorded.

title: C# parser doesn't extract nested types (classes, records, structs)
description:
The C# parser's `extract_class_members` function only extracts methods and constructors from type declarations. It does NOT recurse into nested type declarations (classes, records, structs, interfaces).

**Impact**: References like `Distribution.Percentage` can't be resolved because `Percentage` (a nested record) is never indexed.

**Root cause**: `extract_class_members()` in `csharp.rs:663-696` only handles `METHOD_DECLARATION` and `CONSTRUCTOR_DECLARATION`. It should also handle `CLASS_DECLARATION`, `RECORD_DECLARATION`, `STRUCT_DECLARATION`, `INTERFACE_DECLARATION`.

**Test added**: `extracts_nested_types` test in `csharp.rs` (currently failing) demonstrates the issue.

**Example**:
```csharp
public abstract record Distribution
{
    public sealed record Percentage : Distribution { }  // NOT extracted
    public sealed record FixedAmount : Distribution { } // NOT extracted
}
```

Only `Distribution` is extracted, not the nested `Percentage` or `FixedAmount` types.

title: Compute content hash for indexed files
description:
The schema supports `content_hash` but it's always `None`. See `batch_writer.rs:238`, `lib.rs:643`, `lib.rs:803`.

Content hashing would provide more reliable change detection than mtime/size alone, useful for:
- Detecting changes after file touch without content change
- Cache key generation
- Detecting identical content in different files

title: Type hierarchy: extract and traverse extends/implements edges
description:
Add type-hierarchy queries — walk inheritance and trait/interface implementation chains upward (base types) or downward (derived types).

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's kirograph_type_hierarchy tool.

**Languages:**
- Rust: impl X for Y, trait X: Y + Z, struct X<T: Bound>
- C#: class X : BaseClass, IInterface, IGeneric<T>

**Schema additions:**
- New ReferenceKind variants: Extends, Implements (already partly modeled in ReferenceKind enum — check current state).
- Tree-sitter extractors for both languages emit these edges during indexing.
- Reuse the existing refs table; no new tables needed.

**Why this depends on rivets-aay4:**
For methods, we want to walk ''method M of class X'' to its overridden version in BaseClass. That requires knowing which class a method belongs to, which is parent_symbol_id. Without it, we can do class-to-class hierarchy but not method-to-method override resolution.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::get_type_hierarchy(symbol: &str, direction: HierarchyDirection) -> Result<TypeHierarchy>
- enum HierarchyDirection { Up, Down, Both }

**CLI:**
- tethys hierarchy <SYMBOL> [--direction up|down|both]
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Rust extractor emits Extends/Implements edges for impl blocks and trait bounds
- [ ] C# extractor emits Extends/Implements edges for class inheritance and interface lists
- [ ] get_type_hierarchy() API method
- [ ] CLI subcommand tethys hierarchy
- [ ] Tests on a Rust trait fixture and a C# class-hierarchy fixture
- [ ] MCP tool tethys_type_hierarchy (sibling rivets-o4re)

title: Integrate LSP into index --lsp
description:
When `--lsp` flag is set during indexing:

1. After Pass 2, identify still-unresolved references
2. For each, call LSP goto_definition to find the target
3. Update reference with resolved symbol_id

LSP is spawned lazily, kept alive for batch queries, shutdown on completion.

title: tethys: add exhaustive-byte invariant test for PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET
description:
PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET in crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs is a hand-maintained chain of 27 .add(b'X') calls. A typo (duplicate, missing entry) is silent — the existing format_uri_* tests only spot-check specific inputs (spaces, %20, Unicode).

Add a test that iterates 0u8..=127 and asserts: for every byte b, set.contains(b) == is_must_encode(b), where is_must_encode is the inverse RFC-3986 definition:
  !b.is_ascii_alphanumeric() && !matches!(b, b'-'|b'.'|b'_'|b'~'|b'/'|b':') || b.is_ascii_control()

~10 lines, runs in microseconds, catches every list-drift bug at CI time.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (type-design-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Test added to crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs#tests module
- [ ] Iterates 0..=127 and asserts set membership matches inverse definition
- [ ] cargo nextest run -p tethys passes

title: Populate parent_symbol_id for nested symbols
description:
The schema supports `parent_symbol_id` for hierarchical symbols (e.g., method inside struct) but it's always `None`. See `lib.rs:632` and `lib.rs:761`.

Populating this would enable queries like:
- "Show all methods of struct X"
- "Find all nested types in module Y"
- Symbol hierarchy navigation

title: tethys: replace Mutex<Connection> with a pool; collapse get_package_coupling round-trips
description:
get_package_coupling currently does 3 sequential DB round-trips:
  1. SELECT target package row from arch_coupling JOIN arch_packages
  2. SELECT outgoing neighbors via fetch_neighbors(OUTGOING_SQL)
  3. SELECT incoming neighbors via fetch_neighbors(INCOMING_SQL)

The reason 2 and 3 can't share the connection guard with 1 is the Mutex<Connection> in db/mod.rs is non-reentrant — fetch_neighbors re-acquires the lock internally, so the outer guard from step 1 must drop first. This was the mutex bug caught and documented during PR 60 development.

Reviewer flagged in PR 60 round 5: "If the Mutex<Connection> is ever replaced with a connection pool (e.g. r2d2 or a custom pool), this could be collapsed into fewer round-trips."

This is architecturally the right time to address it — the connection-pool migration would benefit other code paths too (concurrent queries from MCP tools, parallel architecture phase passes, etc.).

Scope:
1. Add a connection pool (r2d2-sqlite or similar) wrapping rusqlite::Connection
2. Either re-architect get_package_coupling to hold one connection across all three queries (single transaction, isolation guarantees) OR fold all three queries into one with UNION + JOIN
3. Remove the documented scope-block workaround in get_package_coupling
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] db/mod.rs uses a connection pool instead of Mutex<Connection>
- [ ] get_package_coupling no longer needs the explicit-drop scope block
- [ ] Benchmark before/after for get_package_coupling on a workspace of >50 crates
- [ ] All existing tests still pass; the mutex non-reentrancy test stays green
- [ ] Document the pool sizing rationale in db/mod.rs

title: Add imports table to schema
Implement incremental index updates
description:
Currently `update()` in `lib.rs:2095-2104` re-indexes everything. The schema stores `mtime_ns` and `size_bytes` which could enable proper incremental updates:

1. Query files from DB
2. Compare mtime/size with filesystem
3. Only re-parse changed files
4. Remove deleted files from index

This would significantly improve performance for large codebases.

title: tethys: implement proper incremental update and staleness check
description: CodeIndex::update() currently re-indexes everything instead of doing a proper incremental update. CodeIndex::needs_update() always returns true. Implement proper staleness detection and incremental re-indexing.

title: Import-usage detection: a method call makes a same-named unused free-fn import look used (phantom file_dep)
description:
compute_dependencies (src/indexing.rs:896) decides an import is 'used' if the imported name appears in the file's referenced names (built from parsed ExtractedReference.name + path prefixes). It does not distinguish a free-function reference from a method call of the same name. So an UNUSED free-fn import collides with a same-named method and produces a phantom file dependency.

Repro:
  src/a.rs: pub fn process() {}
  src/b.rs:
    use crate::a::process;        // never called as a free fn -> unused
    pub struct T; impl T { pub fn process(&self) {} }
    pub fn run(t: &T) { t.process(); }   // method call, name 'process'
  After index: file_deps contains b.rs -> a.rs (PHANTOM; b does not use a::process).

Independent of tethys-6rlu (the refs_named view does not touch dependency computation, which runs in Pass 1 from parsed names before resolution). Same name-collision class as tethys-53iv but in the import-usage/file_deps layer rather than ref resolution. K-hybrid corroboration (file_deps) may already mitigate some cases; this fixture shows a residual.

Impact: over-reported file dependencies / coupling edges; false cycles possible.
acceptance_criteria:
* In the repro, no b.rs -> a.rs file_dep is produced (the import is correctly unused).
* A method call does not count as a use of a same-named free-function import.
* Legitimate import-usage detection (free-fn import actually called) unchanged (regression).

title: Implement Pass 2: resolve references against symbol DB
description:
After all files are indexed, run a second pass to resolve unresolved references:

1. Query all references with symbol_id = NULL
2. For each, look up import context from imports table
3. Match reference name against symbols from imported modules
4. Update reference with resolved symbol_id

Resolution priority:
1. Same-file lookup
2. Explicit import lookup
3. Qualified path resolution
4. Glob import search
5. Leave unresolved (LSP candidate)

title: MCP server for tethys tools
description:
Expose tethys functionality as MCP tools for AI agent integration. AI assistants like Claude/Cursor/Copilot can then query the indexed graph instead of grepping files, dramatically cutting tool-call count and context usage on long sessions.

Inspired by both Drift (50+ MCP tools across 7 layers) and KiroGraph (16 MCP tools, all auto-approved). KiroGraph's design observation drives most of the value: ''Kiro literally queries the graph instead of grepping files,'' so the index becomes context the model uses for every prompt.

**MVP tools (wrap existing Tethys API methods):**

| Tool | Description | Underlying API |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| tethys_status | Index stats, language breakdown | get_stats |
| tethys_symbol | Get symbol by qualified name | get_symbol |
| tethys_search | Search symbols by name/kind | search_symbols |
| tethys_callers | Who calls this symbol? | get_callers |
| tethys_callees | What does this symbol call? | get_symbol_dependencies |
| tethys_impact | Impact analysis for a symbol | get_symbol_impact |
| tethys_file_impact | Impact analysis for a file | get_impact |
| tethys_reachable | Forward/backward reachability | get_forward_reachable / get_backward_reachable |
| tethys_cycles | Detect dependency cycles | detect_cycles |
| tethys_panic_points | Find .unwrap()/.expect() calls | get_panic_points |
| tethys_affected_tests | Tests affected by changed files | get_affected_tests |

**v2 tools (depend on new analytics — see sibling issues):**

| Tool | Description | Depends on |
|------|-------------|------------|
| tethys_path | Shortest path between two symbols | new path-query feature |
| tethys_dead_code | Symbols with zero incoming refs | new dead-code feature |
| tethys_hotspots | Most-connected symbols | new hotspots feature |
| tethys_surprising | Non-obvious cross-file edges | new surprising-connections feature |
| tethys_type_hierarchy | Up/down extends-implements walk | new type-hierarchy feature |
| tethys_coupling | Per-package Ca/Ce/instability | new architecture-metrics feature |
| tethys_diff | Graph diff vs. saved snapshot | new snapshot/diff feature |

**Implementation plan:**
- New crates/tethys-mcp crate (binary + thin lib).
- Use rmcp (already in workspace via rivets-mcp).
- Each tool is a thin async wrapper around a Tethys method.
- Tools should default to auto-approved in the Claude MCP config so the model can call them freely.
- Coexists with rivets-mcp on a single MCP host.

**Benefits:**
- AI agents query codebase structure instead of grepping files.
- Integrates with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, and any MCP-compatible client.
- Complements rivets-mcp (issue tracker) and tethys's CLI.

title: Store imports during indexing (Rust + C#)
description:
During file indexing, extract import statements and store them in the imports table.

For Rust: parse `use` statements via existing `extract_use_statements()`
For C#: parse `using` directives via existing `extract_using_directives()`

Map both to the common imports table format.

title: Implement deferred dependency resolution for circular dependencies
description:
Currently, dependency detection fails silently when the target file hasn't been indexed yet. This happens with circular dependencies (A→B, B→A) where the first-indexed file's dependency on the second file is lost.

**Current behavior**: Dependencies can only be recorded to files already in the database. Files indexed earlier may not have their dependencies to later-indexed files recorded.

**Proposed solution**: Instead of two full passes, queue unresolved dependencies and retry after the initial pass:

1. First pass: Index all files, queue dependencies that fail to resolve (target not in DB)
2. Resolution passes: Retry pending queue until it stops shrinking
3. Log any remaining unresolved (external crates or truly missing files)

**Convergence check**: `pending.len() < prev_count` ensures we don't loop forever.

**Benefits**:
- More efficient than re-indexing everything
- Handles circular dependencies elegantly
- Minimal code change to existing `index()` flow
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] `PendingDependency` struct created

title: tethys: expand file_deps test coverage (rename, target deletion, DB-unit, rebuild idempotency)
description:
Surfaced by pr-test-analyzer during multi-agent review of PR #65. The 2 regression tests added in rivets-lcb6 fence the specific reported bug well, but four adjacent code paths are uncovered.

Tests to add (in crates/tethys/tests/file_deps_idempotency.rs unless noted):

1. file_deps_swapped_when_use_target_changes (rating 8 / high). Replace 'use crate_target::Widget' with 'use crate_target_alt::Gadget' in the fixture. Re-index. Assert the edge to crate_target is GONE and the edge to crate_target_alt EXISTS — check by exact FileId lookup, not just row count (counts could net to zero coincidentally and hide the bug).

2. clear_all_file_deps unit test (rating 6 / medium). crates/tethys/src/db/file_deps.rs has no #[cfg(test)] mod tests block. clear_all_file_deps is pub and per CLAUDE.md 'every public method needs tests' (rust-best-practices Rule 39). Add: insert 2 deps via insert_file_dependency, call clear_all_file_deps, assert get_file_dependencies returns [] for both source FileIds. Catches SQL typos / schema renames the integration tests would miss.

3. file_deps_target_file_deletion_removes_edge (rating 8 / high but DEFERRED to rivets-dhxo). Delete crate_target/src/lib.rs from disk, re-index, assert no edges reference the orphan file. This test would currently FAIL in streaming mode — that's the whole point. Defer landing this until rivets-dhxo is fixed so it doesn't break CI. Cross-reference here so the regression-fence test gets added alongside the fix.

4. rebuild_with_options idempotency (rating 7 / medium). The design doc notes rebuild is 'correct by accident' because db.reset() wipes the table first. Add a #[case::rebuild] variant or separate test calling rebuild_with_options(IndexOptions::default()) twice and assert FileDepsSnapshot stability. Defends against someone 'optimizing' rebuild to skip the reset later.

5. (Suggestion, rating 4) file with 2 uses of same target → remove one → row remains, ref_count drops. Probes the multi-ref-per-edge code path the existing tests don't exercise.

Source: PR #65 multi-agent review aggregate (pr-test-analyzer findings 1-5).
acceptance_criteria:
* file_deps_swapped_when_use_target_changes added and passing
* clear_all_file_deps unit test added to db/file_deps.rs #[cfg(test)] mod tests
* rebuild_with_options idempotency test added (as #[case::rebuild] or standalone)
* (deferred) file_deps_target_file_deletion_removes_edge added once rivets-dhxo lands

title: Snapshot and diff: track structural graph changes over time
description:
Save lightweight snapshots of the graph and compare them to current state. Useful before/after refactors, in CI to audit what a PR added or removed structurally, and as a baseline for regression detection.

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's snapshot save / snapshot diff / kirograph_diff MCP tool. Storage is just JSON arrays of node IDs and edge tuples; diff is set-difference, O(n) regardless of codebase size.

**Implementation:**
- Storage: .rivets/snapshots/{label}.json with serialized list of (qualified_name, kind) for nodes and (caller_qn, callee_qn, kind) for edges. JSON over native binary because they're small and human-readable.
- Default snapshot label is the timestamp; named labels (e.g. 'pre-refactor') are first-class.
- Diff is computed as two set differences: added = current - snapshot, removed = snapshot - current.
- Public API:
  - Tethys::save_snapshot(label) -> Result<SnapshotMeta>
  - Tethys::list_snapshots() -> Result<Vec<SnapshotMeta>>
  - Tethys::diff_snapshot(label) -> Result<SnapshotDiff>
- CLI:
  - tethys snapshot save [LABEL]
  - tethys snapshot list
  - tethys snapshot diff [LABEL] [--format full|summary|json]

**CI use case:**
gh pr create … then tethys snapshot save pre-pr; merge; tethys snapshot diff pre-pr to audit structural changes.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] save_snapshot, list_snapshots, diff_snapshot API methods
- [ ] CLI subcommands snapshot save / list / diff
- [ ] Snapshot file format documented (JSON schema in module-level docs)
- [ ] Diff returns added_symbols, removed_symbols, added_edges, removed_edges
- [ ] Default label is timestamp; named labels supported
- [ ] Round-trip test: save → diff against unchanged graph returns zero changes

title: tethys: test format_uri percent-encodes literal % in paths
description:
PATH_PERCENT_ENCODE_SET includes .add(b'%'), so a literal % in a path encodes as %25. No test exercises this. A regression removing .add(b'%') would produce a URI that parses but resolves to the wrong file (or double-decodes downstream).

Rare in Rust source paths but load-bearing for the RFC 3986 round-trip claim.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (pr-test-analyzer).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Test added to crates/tethys/src/lsp/transport.rs#tests
- [ ] Input path contains a literal % character
- [ ] Asserts the % is encoded as %25 in the resulting URI

title: Add CSharpLsProvider
Per-language display spelling for qualified names at query/output boundary
description: tethys stores qualified names with a canonical `::` separator for all languages (a cache format, decided in .separator-fix/spec.md decision #5). C# consumers see `AuthService::Login` in CLI/library output where C# syntax would be `AuthService.Login`. Add a display-formatting layer at the query/output boundary that re-spells qualified names per the symbol's language. Storage stays canonical `::`; this is presentation only. Deferred from the separator-fix loop (see .separator-fix/spec.md, probe findings in .separator-fix/probe-findings.md).

title: tethys: test format_uri returns Ok for nonexistent path (I/O-free seam)
description:
The path_to_uri / format_uri split exposes a contract that format_uri is I/O-free (its rustdoc says 'without performing any filesystem I/O'). The existing test path_to_uri_returns_invalid_path_for_nonexistent only verifies the wrapper still surfaces canonicalize errors — it doesn't pin down the seam itself.

Add a test asserting format_uri(Path::new(r'C:\does\not\exist\foo.rs')) returns Ok. If someone later 'fixes' format_uri by re-introducing a metadata() or canonicalize() call, that test catches the regression and forces them to explain.

Surfaced by PR #64 round-3 review (pr-test-analyzer).

title: Implement parent_symbol_id for nested symbol tracking
description: Two TODO sites in lib.rs (lines 691, 823) pass None for parent_symbol_id. Implementing this would enable tracking nested symbols (methods within impl blocks, nested functions) for more precise dependency graphs.
notes: Closed: Duplicate of rivets-aay4 (older, describes the same parent_symbol_id TODOs in lib.rs).

title: tethys: define and document --depth 0 semantics for impact analysis
description:
After PR #57, `tethys impact --depth N` is wired through. `--depth 0` is currently undefined behavior: it would call `get_transitive_dependents(file_id, Some(0))` which depends on whatever the SQLite recursive CTE does at depth 0 (likely returns nothing).

Decide and document the contract: either treat 0 as 'direct dependents only and no transitive search', or reject it at the CLI layer with a friendly error. Add a test pinning the behavior either way.

title: Auto-sync via dirty-marker + deferred flush
description:
Add a low-overhead pattern for keeping the tethys index fresh during active editing: file-save writes a tiny dirty marker (cheap, no parse), and the actual re-sync runs at session boundaries (e.g. agent stop, pre-prompt).

**Inspired by:** KiroGraph's hook system. The pattern avoids per-keystroke overhead while keeping the graph fresh enough that AI assistants always see current code structure.

**Two-phase flow:**

Phase 1 — mark dirty (cheap):
- A new tethys mark-dirty subcommand writes <workspace>/.rivets/index/dirty.txt (one path per line, append-only).
- O(1) — no parsing, no SQL writes, suitable for per-save invocation from an editor or hook.

Phase 2 — sync if dirty (deferred):
- A new tethys sync-if-dirty subcommand reads dirty.txt, re-indexes only the listed files (incremental), and clears the marker.
- Suitable for invocation at agent stop, pre-prompt, or other session boundaries.

**Hook integration:**
- Claude Code: PostToolUse hook on Edit/Write writes via mark-dirty; Stop hook runs sync-if-dirty.
- Editors: file-save hooks invoke mark-dirty.
- CI: a pre-test step runs sync-if-dirty to ensure the index reflects HEAD.

**Why this depends on rivets-q8qw and rivets-3l14:**
- rivets-q8qw (incremental updates): without it, the sync re-indexes the whole workspace, defeating the deferred-flush optimization.
- rivets-3l14 (content hash): mtime alone is not a reliable change indicator (touch updates mtime without changing content). Content hash makes ''actually changed'' the trigger.

**Public API:**
- Tethys::mark_dirty(paths: &[Path]) -> Result<()>
- Tethys::sync_if_dirty() -> Result<IndexUpdate>
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] mark_dirty and sync_if_dirty API methods
- [ ] CLI subcommands tethys mark-dirty PATH... and tethys sync-if-dirty [--quiet]
- [ ] Dirty marker format documented (newline-separated relative paths, dedup on read)
- [ ] sync-if-dirty is a no-op when marker is absent or empty
- [ ] Race-safe: mark-dirty appends with lock; sync-if-dirty reads-then-truncates atomically
- [ ] Example Claude Code hook config in tethys README
- [ ] Tests cover: empty marker, deleted file in marker, concurrent mark + sync

Enable tethys to resolve references across files, so `tethys callers` and `tethys impact` work for symbols defined in other files.
